data: Low level of health literacy awareness and skills in organisations, communiy and voluntary sector, unsupportive policies
What are the causes of the problem?
System level processes and targets not set
Building health literacy understanding and skills in organisations through awareness and training, supporting more health literate policies - HL in all systems, review of systems
What actions are involved in doing something about this?
Health literacy awareness training. 1. Goal setting for system i.e. number (%) organisations trained. 2. Frequency of training
How will we deliver these actions?
Accredited training accessed Face to Face or online
What do we expect to come out of these actions?
Increased awareness of health literacy using post training questionnaire
How will we know things have changed?
What short term changes will we see?
What do we think will happen in the longer term (3 years)? For example, increased health literacy, better disease control, improved health
Number of organisations up to date with training
In-depth review of organisational systems from a HL perspective
Audit process in a webinar / face to face setting e.g. MPOHL ORG HL
1. Understanding of areas that need to improve e.g. communications, website
What are the current activities in this area?
Different systems (like health and social services) use different words for things
People who run the different systems
A range of different words used by different people
Systems that do not talk to each other
Systems that are not joined up
using words that are easy to understand
making sure letters to patients and the public are easy to understand
letting people tell us when words are too complex
systems that can be changed if needed
knowing all the different words and what they mean
agreeing on a common list of words across our region
making sure these words are easy to understand
making sure systems are in place to write information that is easy to understand and use simple words
develop data systems that talk with each other
develop a set of words that everyone agrees on
make sure everyone knows what these words mean
training courses on what words to use
better patient and public understanding of materials
information that is more sensitive to different cultures
feedback from people who use the system
We will have action plans in place
systems will work better for patients
Information that is easier to understand
systems using the same words as each other
systems that are easier to use
Health information is too hard to read and has too much jargon
Organisations don't know there is a problem
Staff in organisations don't know there is a problem
Low staff skills in health literacy
Lack of staff time to rewrite materials
Lack of resources to rewrite materials
Not currently part of the culture of organisations
Organisation systems not fully accessible to staff
Online and automated tools to help rewrite documents
Better data systems to safely store information
Using pictures, diagrams, videos and animations
making the IT systems easier to use
Rewriting materials so that they are easy to read
improving systems to include health literacy
giving staff training in health literacy
Making sure staff are trained often enough
working with graphic illustrators, film makers and animation makers
Checking how complex materials are now (an audit)
Develop IT tools to aid simplifying health information
Authorised training including online and face to face
work with people who can develop video and audio alternatives
always explain codes and jargon used in materials
health information that is easier to read and use
Health information available in other languages
Less anxiety for people reading and using information
Asking patients and the public what they think
Describing what patients and the public think
Higher number of languages in which health information is available
Higher number and percentage of materials written in clear language
Self-assessment questionnaires filled in by patients, members of the public and staff
We will be developing action plans
Systems will work better for people
Staff will be happier and more engaged
People will have better skills for health
Systems will be easier to navigate
Materials will be easier to use
Materials that are easier to use
Better use of GP and hospital appointments
Improved health literacy of the public
Health and public health professionals are not trained in health literacy
The social work regulation authority (Social Work England)
Lack of awareness of the need for health literacy in traning for teaching and social work professionals
Meeting with key people (ministers, civil servants, regulatory bodies)
Health literacy training embedded in health and public health professional training
Health literacy will appear in health and public health training
More health and public health professions will be starting to learn about health literacy as they train
We know more about the health of some groups of people than others.
People who are at risk or outside everyday society (e.g. homeless people)
People in care or who have left care
People who have been in the armed forces
People with learning or development disabilities
People who receive or give unpaid care
People who are in the country illegally
People who look after health and social care data
People who do not turn up for appointments
We don't always get information about people who:
are receiving or giving unfunded care
find it hard to get care
do not have a home address
People and teams who provide services working together
Organisations having good policies and systems in place
People who provide care being friendly and welcoming
Having services that people can use
Developing health information in other languages
Working with people who teach English
Mapping out the teams and organisations that provide services
Working with people who provide services
Working with people who receive services
Telling people what they are entitled to
Helping people claim what they are entitled to
Teaching people how to use the systems
Supporting people to become more active in their own health
Develop data systems that talk to each other
More people will be included in datasets
Clear use of language and terms across systems
Higher number (and proportion) of previously excluded people on databases
Being able to measure health of more people (EQ5D)
Being able to describe levels of illness in more people
Being able to measure wellbeing of more people (Edinburgh wellbeing scale)
We will know more about what different populations need
We will have action plans to deliver better services
We will know how we are going to monitor and evaluate action plans
Systems that are easier to use
People find it tricky to do things like reading, counting looking after money.
People from vulnerable groups like disabled people
Teachers for speakers of other languages able to teach reading counting and money skills alongside English
People having better understanding of reading, writing, counting and money management

people able to understand more about their health

people will manage their health conditions better
people will be happier with the services they receive

Health and local authority / social care organisations will know about health literacy
Training courses will be in place
people will have fewer worries about money.
Schools that did not teach properly
Teachers who can teach reading, counting, writing and money skills
Teaching reading, writing, counting and money skills to people who want to learn
Making teaching courses available to people who need them

Face to face support, reading groups set up
Teaching reading, writing, counting and money skills to staff
Organisations to offer staff training to build staff reading, writing, counting and money skills
Health and local authority systems can't be understood and used by people with low skills
Staff training in consultation tools e.g. Teach back, Chunk & Check
Staff & administrator training in using readability tool
Actions at the level of organisation to ensure materials can be read and used by people with all levels of skills
Staff trained to help people use the health and benefits systems
Using different ways if giving people information like TVs in waiting rooms, online videos
Training seen as an essential part of work

greater trust between patients / clients and health and local authority organisations
